title: The tool library on Maple Street
summary: A small-town tool library in Oregon set out to share equipment. Its borrowing records turned out to be a quiet map of who in town could not afford what.
body:
On Maple Street, in a town in southern Oregon with a population of about nine thousand, there is a tool library. It occupies the back half of an old fire station. Members pay nine dollars a year. The library lends about four hundred items — drills, ladders, sewing machines, post-hole diggers, a couple of carpet cleaners, two car-battery chargers, and one industrial-grade dehydrator. It opened in 2014. It has more than nine hundred active members now, which is, in a town of nine thousand, a lot.

The librarian is a retired electrician named June Fairweather. She keeps a paper logbook, although she also has a small computer. She has been writing down every borrowing for ten years. She did this, originally, only because the insurance asked her to. After about four years she noticed that the logbook had become a quieter kind of document.

The pattern she noticed was simple. Certain items were borrowed almost only by people on the eastern edge of town, where the houses are smaller and older. The carpet cleaners, the sewing machines, the post-hole diggers, and the two car-battery chargers were all in that group. Other items — the high-end drills, the laser level, the wallpaper steamer — circulated mostly in the newer neighbourhoods to the west. The dehydrator went, by far, mostly to one elderly couple who preserved their own food because their pension did not stretch to fresh vegetables in February.

'I realised,' June told me, 'that the borrowing list is a list of what people can't afford to own. Nobody borrows a thing they could just buy.' She paused. 'I had spent four years thinking of this as a generous neighbourhood project. I had not understood that it was also a quiet record of who in this town is poor.'

The realisation changed how she ran the place. She stopped, gently, listing the most-borrowed items in the monthly newsletter, because that list was effectively a list of which households were struggling. She kept the data, but she stopped publishing it. She also began, when grant applications asked for 'success stories', to write differently. She no longer wrote about how many drills had been lent. She wrote, instead, about how many cold winters the car-battery chargers had got people through.

She has also changed the rules in small ways. The dehydrator, which had a two-week limit, can now be kept for the whole autumn by the elderly couple. The carpet cleaners, which used to require a deposit, no longer do. June expected pushback from the board. There was none.

Last year a graduate student in social policy asked to study the logbook. June agreed, on one condition: no household could be named. The student's report — published quietly, in a journal nobody in town reads — argues that lending libraries, when properly run, function as 'unembarrassed welfare'. They give people things they need without forcing them to declare that they need them. June read the paper. She said the phrase was right, but a little cold. She prefers to think of it as 'the town being polite to itself'.

annotations.meaningVi: thư viện cho mượn dụng cụ
an sinh không gây xấu hổ

annotations.phrase: the town being polite to itself
annotations.meaningVi: thành phố lịch sự với chính mình

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for accumulated experience
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng present perfect (have/has + V3) cho điều bắt đầu trong quá khứ, tiếp tục đến hiện tại, hoặc đã tích luỹ. Trong bài: 'She has been writing down every borrowing for ten years.' (present perfect continuous — kéo dài 10 năm tới nay). 'It has more than nine hundred active members now.' (kết quả tích luỹ tới hiện tại). 'She has also changed the rules in small ways.' (kết quả gần đây). 'No household could be named.' (modal quá khứ).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses present perfect (have/has + V3) for actions started in the past, continuing now or accumulated. In the text: 'She has been writing down every borrowing for ten years.' (present perfect continuous — sustained 10 years to now). 'It has more than nine hundred active members now.' (accumulated result up to now). 'She has also changed the rules in small ways.' (recent accumulated change). 'No household could be named.' (past modal of permission).

translationVi:
Trên phố Maple, ở một thị trấn nam Oregon dân số khoảng chín nghìn, có một thư viện cho mượn dụng cụ. Nó chiếm nửa sau của một trạm cứu hoả cũ. Thành viên trả chín đô-la một năm. Thư viện cho mượn khoảng bốn trăm món — máy khoan, thang, máy may, dụng cụ đào lỗ cọc, vài máy giặt thảm, hai bộ sạc ắc-quy ô-tô, và một máy sấy thực phẩm cỡ công nghiệp. Mở năm 2014. Nay có hơn chín trăm thành viên đang hoạt động, là rất nhiều ở một thị trấn chín nghìn dân.

Thủ thư là một thợ điện đã nghỉ hưu tên June Fairweather. Bà giữ một cuốn sổ giấy, dù có máy tính nhỏ. Bà đã ghi từng lượt mượn mười năm. Ban đầu chỉ vì bảo hiểm yêu cầu. Sau bốn năm bà nhận ra cuốn sổ đã trở thành một loại tài liệu lặng lẽ khác.

Mô hình bà nhận ra rất đơn giản. Một số món gần như chỉ được mượn bởi người sống phía đông thị trấn, nơi nhà nhỏ hơn và cũ hơn. Máy giặt thảm, máy may, dụng cụ đào lỗ cọc, hai bộ sạc ắc-quy đều thuộc nhóm đó. Những món khác — máy khoan cao cấp, máy laser cân bằng, máy bóc giấy dán tường — chủ yếu lưu hành ở các khu mới phía tây. Máy sấy thực phẩm, áp đảo, đa phần đến tay một cặp vợ chồng già tự bảo quản thực phẩm vì lương hưu không đủ mua rau tươi tháng Hai.

'Tôi nhận ra,' June nói với tôi, 'danh sách mượn là danh sách những thứ người ta không đủ tiền sở hữu. Không ai mượn một thứ mà họ có thể mua dễ dàng.' Bà ngừng. 'Tôi đã bốn năm nghĩ nơi này là một dự án hào phóng của khu phố. Tôi chưa hiểu rằng nó cũng là một bản ghi lặng lẽ về việc ai trong thị trấn này nghèo.'

Nhận thức đó thay đổi cách bà điều hành. Bà nhẹ nhàng ngừng liệt kê các món được mượn nhiều nhất trong bản tin hàng tháng, vì danh sách đó thực chất là danh sách các hộ đang chật vật. Bà giữ dữ liệu, nhưng ngừng công bố. Bà cũng bắt đầu, khi đơn xin tài trợ hỏi 'câu chuyện thành công', viết khác đi. Bà không còn viết về số máy khoan đã cho mượn. Bà viết về bao nhiêu mùa đông lạnh mà các bộ sạc ắc-quy đã giúp người ta vượt qua.

Bà cũng thay đổi quy định một cách nhỏ. Máy sấy, vốn giới hạn hai tuần, nay có thể giữ cả mùa thu cho cặp vợ chồng già. Máy giặt thảm, từng yêu cầu đặt cọc, không cần nữa. June đoán ban điều hành sẽ phản đối. Không ai phản đối.

Năm ngoái một nghiên cứu sinh chính sách xã hội xin nghiên cứu cuốn sổ. June đồng ý với một điều kiện: không được nêu tên hộ nào. Báo cáo của sinh viên — đăng lặng trong một tạp chí không ai trong thị trấn đọc — lập luận rằng thư viện cho mượn, khi vận hành tốt, hoạt động như 'an sinh không gây xấu hổ'. Nó cho người ta thứ họ cần mà không bắt họ tuyên bố là cần. June đã đọc. Bà nói cụm từ ấy đúng, nhưng hơi lạnh. Bà thích nghĩ về nó như 'thành phố đang lịch sự với chính mình'.

questions.prompt: Where is the library and how big is membership?
options: Back of an old fire station on Maple Street in southern Oregon; >900 members in a town of ~9,000
June Fairweather, a retired electrician, who has kept a paper logbook for ten years

questions.prompt: Why did she originally keep the logbook?
options: Because the insurance asked her to
Because she had to publish it

questions.prompt: Which items were borrowed mostly on the eastern (older, smaller-house) side?
options: Carpet cleaners, sewing machines, post-hole diggers, car-battery chargers
An elderly couple whose pension didn't stretch to fresh winter vegetables — they preserved their own food
The borrowing list is a list of what people can't afford to own — a quiet record of who in town is poor

questions.prompt: What did she stop doing in the newsletter, and why?
options: Stopped publishing the most-borrowed items list, because it effectively named struggling households

questions.prompt: How did she change grant 'success stories'?
options: No longer counted drills lent; instead, how many cold winters the car-battery chargers got people through

questions.prompt: What rule changes did she make?
options: Dehydrator can be kept for the whole autumn by the elderly couple; carpet cleaners no longer need a deposit

questions.prompt: What did she expect from the board, and what happened?
What did the graduate student argue?
options: Properly run lending libraries function as 'unembarrassed welfare' — needs met without declaration
A community lending project, treated only as 'sharing', is also a quiet record of need — and a good librarian can keep that record without exposing the people it describes.
Poor towns should not have libraries.
questions.hint: Look at June's realisation and rule changes.

cloze.template:
On Maple Street, in a town in southern Oregon with a population of about nine thousand, there is a {{b1}} library. It occupies the back half of an old fire station. Members pay nine dollars a year. The library lends about four hundred items — drills, ladders, sewing machines, post-hole diggers, a couple of carpet cleaners, two car-battery chargers, and one industrial-grade dehydrator. It opened in 2014. It has more than {{b2}} active members now, which is, in a town of nine thousand, a lot.

The librarian is a retired {{b3}} named June Fairweather. She keeps a paper logbook, although she also has a small computer. She has been writing down every borrowing for ten years. She did this, originally, only because the {{b4}} asked her to. After about four years she noticed that the logbook had become a quieter kind of document.

The pattern she noticed was simple. Certain items were borrowed almost only by people on the {{b5}} edge of town, where the houses are smaller and older. The carpet cleaners, the sewing machines, the post-hole diggers, and the two car-battery chargers were all in that group. Other items — the high-end drills, the laser level, the wallpaper steamer — circulated mostly in the newer neighbourhoods to the west. The dehydrator went, by far, mostly to one elderly couple who preserved their own food because their {{b6}} did not stretch to fresh vegetables in February.

'I realised,' June told me, 'that the borrowing list is a list of what people can't afford to {{b7}}. Nobody borrows a thing they could just buy.' She paused. 'I had spent four years thinking of this as a generous neighbourhood project. I had not understood that it was also a quiet record of who in this town is {{b8}}.'

The realisation changed how she ran the place. She stopped, gently, listing the most-borrowed items in the monthly {{b9}}, because that list was effectively a list of which households were struggling. She kept the data, but she stopped publishing it. She also began, when grant applications asked for 'success stories', to write differently. She no longer wrote about how many drills had been lent. She wrote, instead, about how many cold winters the car-battery chargers had got people through.

She has also changed the rules in small ways. The dehydrator, which had a two-week limit, can now be kept for the whole autumn by the elderly couple. The carpet cleaners, which used to require a {{b10}}, no longer do. June expected pushback from the board. There was none.

Last year a graduate student in social policy asked to study the logbook. June agreed, on one condition: no household could be named. The student's report — published quietly, in a journal nobody in town reads — argues that lending libraries, when properly run, function as 'unembarrassed {{b11}}'. They give people things they need without forcing them to declare that they need them. June read the paper. She said the phrase was right, but a little cold. She prefers to think of it as 'the town being polite to itself'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: June chose to keep the data but stop publishing it, because 'most-borrowed' was the same as 'most struggling'. When you have useful information about people in your community, what's the difference between sharing it and exposing them?
